links.mailtoSubject: People v. Tyler Nashae Thompson - demand for maximum prosecution and full transparency
links.mailtoBody:
Dear Orange County District Attorney Team,

I am writing regarding People v. Tyler Nashae Thompson, charged with violations of Penal Code 597(a) PC, 597(b) PC, and 135 PC for throwing a severely neglected poodle in a plastic bag over the eight foot fence of the University Veterinary Center on Glenoaks Avenue in Anaheim.

The facts in your own public statements and in media coverage are not ambiguous.

Surveillance video shows Ms. Thompson throwing a plastic grocery bag containing a small white poodle over an eight foot fence into the back yard of the veterinary clinic, then walking back into a nearby apartment building. When the bag hits the ground, the dog staggers out.

The veterinarian later reported that the dog was extremely malnourished, severely anemic, infested with fleas, missing a tooth, with nails so overgrown they had curled into the pads, and suffering trauma, severe bruising, and facial injuries from the fall. In plain language: she starved him, neglected him, then threw him.

Yes, the dog was thrown into the yard of a veterinary clinic rather than into a dumpster or the street. That does not magically turn this into an act of compassion. Ms. Thompson could have walked through the front door, rung a bell, or surrendered the dog properly. Instead, she chose to hurl an emaciated, injured animal, sealed in a plastic bag, over an eight foot fence, causing further injury in the process. That is violence, not "surrender."

You have repeatedly stated that animal abuse in Orange County "will be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law" and that only the "truly depraved" would treat a dog this way. Now is the moment to prove that those words are more than press release language.

The wider context is exactly why this case matters.

The FBI now tracks animal cruelty as a Group A offense in the National Incident Based Reporting System, alongside arson, assault, homicide, kidnapping and other serious crimes, because animal cruelty is recognized as a key marker for dangerous offending.

Research summarized by the Animal Legal Defense Fund and others has found animal abuse in a very high percentage of homes where physical child abuse is being investigated, and that animal abusers are several times more likely to commit violent crimes against people.

Studies of offenders charged with animal cruelty show extremely high rates of other criminal charges on their records, including interpersonal violence and sex offenses. New analysis of FBI data shows that animal cruelty frequently co occurs with intimate partner and family violence, including offenses against children.

In plain terms: animal cruelty is not an isolated "pet" issue. It is a red flag crime for future violence, including violence against children. Women offenders are not an exception. If anything, their easier access to children makes ignoring these red flags even more reckless.

Given the facts of this case and the national data, I am asking for the following:

1. That your office seek conviction on both felony counts under Penal Code 597(a) and 597(b), and the misdemeanor count under Penal Code 135, without reduction to misdemeanors or informal "time served" resolutions.

2. That you do not offer, and do not accept, any plea agreement that:

   * dismisses the felony cruelty charges,
   * reduces them to a single misdemeanor,
   * or results in no meaningful custodial sentence.

3. That you request sentencing conditions reflecting the actual harm and risk presented:

   * a substantial custodial term within the statutory maximum,
   * a long term ban on owning, possessing, or living with animals,
   * mandatory psychological evaluation and treatment,
   * and full restitution for the veterinary care and ongoing treatment required to undo the damage she caused.

4. That the Orange County District Attorney’s Office issue clear, public press releases following the next two court dates in this case, the pretrial hearing currently set for December 9 and the preliminary hearing set for December 11, detailing:

   * what occurred in court,
   * what charges remain,
   * and what the next steps are.
     This case began in the public eye; it should not disappear into a sealed back room deal.

5. That the Orange County District Attorney’s Office publicly confirms that cases like this will not be quietly pled down out of sight, and that animal cruelty will be treated in practice as the serious predictor crime that the FBI and decades of research have identified.

Throwing an emaciated dog in a plastic bag over an eight foot fence is not "a mistake," "poor judgment," or a private matter between a pet owner and a vet. It is calculated cruelty toward a defenseless animal. If that does not result in a serious felony conviction and meaningful consequences in Orange County, then your public commitments about animal abuse "not being tolerated" are empty.

Please ensure that People v. Tyler Nashae Thompson is the case that makes it unmistakably clear that this county will not minimize, excuse, or bargain away this level of cruelty, and that the public will be fully informed at each stage.

Sincerely,

links.title: Abused Elephant in Uttar Pradesh
links.descriptionLong:
Location: Bhatwaliya, Ghazipur district, Uttar Pradesh, 233226

This lame captive elephant is being marched down Ghazipur–Kasimabad Road in Bhatwaliya, UP 233226.
His front right leg is visibly injured, possibly from an old fracture that healed incorrectly or severe chronic foot disease.

links.mailtoSubject: Lame captive elephant on Ghazipur–Kasimabad Road (Bhatwaliya, UP 233226) - investigation and seizure requested
links.mailtoBody:
To whom it may concern,

Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing to file a formal complaint regarding a captive elephant being marched through traffic on the Ghazipur–Kasimabad Road in the village of Bhatwaliya, Ghazipur district, Uttar Pradesh, PIN 233226.

A recent video, widely circulating online, shows a large adult Asian elephant being walked along this road in the rain, surrounded by vehicles and people. The animal is heavily burdened with cloth and equipment, and appears to be under the control of at least two men walking beside it. This is not a brief crossing of the road; it appears to be a deliberate use of the elephant for work or money-making in mixed traffic.

Most disturbing, the elephant is visibly lame. One foreleg and foot appear deformed and swollen. The animal is loading the limb at an abnormal angle, rolling onto the inside of the foot instead of placing it flat. With every step, the joints and soft tissue are clearly being shock-loaded on wet tarmac. Forcing a lame elephant to walk like this is not “tradition” or “devotion” – it is blatant cruelty. A still image from the video, focusing on the damaged leg and foot, is attached for your reference.

India calls elephants sacred. Forcing a lame captive elephant to limp down a busy road for entertainment or profit is the exact opposite of sacred. It is dangerous, it is cruel, and it is very likely illegal.

Under the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972, and the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960, I request an immediate, on-the-ground investigation into this elephant and the people controlling it.

Specifically, I request that the authorities:

1. Identify and locate the elephant and its handlers

   * Visit the stretch of Ghazipur–Kasimabad Road passing through Bhatwaliya (PIN 233226).
   * Identify the owner, mahouts, and any contractor or temple claiming custody of this elephant.

2. Verify all legal documents and permissions

   * Ownership certificate and registration for the elephant.
   * Any transport permits, performance permissions, and records of veterinary inspection.
   * Written confirmation that this elephant is properly registered with the Forest Department and conforms to all Wildlife Protection Act requirements.

3. Conduct a full veterinary examination

   * A government veterinarian should examine the elephant specifically for lameness, foot problems, joint damage, infections, or signs of chronic pain and overwork.
   * The findings should be documented in writing, with photographs, as potential evidence.

4. Register an FIR if any violations are found

   * If there is cruelty, lack of valid paperwork, illegal use of the elephant for begging or commercial performances, or any breach of the Wildlife Protection Act or PCA Act, an FIR should be registered against the owner and handlers.
   * Appropriate sections should be invoked, including seizure of the elephant, not just a verbal warning.

5. Remove the elephant from further exploitation

   * If there is evidence of cruelty or illegal use, the elephant should be seized and transferred to a recognised rescue or rehabilitation facility, not simply sent back to the same people with instructions to "be more careful".
   * The individuals responsible should be prohibited from keeping or using elephants in the future.

Right now, the message being sent is that anyone can parade a suffering, lame elephant up and down a busy road for money and no one in authority will intervene. That is unacceptable for a country that claims to respect wildlife and calls elephants sacred.

I request that your office treat this as an urgent matter of animal welfare and public safety. At a minimum, there should be:

* A site visit to Bhatwaliya, Ghazipur–Kasimabad Road, PIN 233226
* Verification of the elephant's legal status and condition
* Clear action taken if any illegality or cruelty is found

Please ensure that this elephant is not quietly marched back out onto the road once the immediate attention has passed.

Evidence: https://drive.google.com/file/d/15DfqZm3zCuB176o2B8WSij-dqleNCY9d/view?usp=sharing

Sincerely,

links.mailtoSubject: Crueldad animal en Eterazama/Animal cruelty in Eterazama
links.mailtoBody:
A quien corresponda,

Les escribo para presentar una denuncia formal por crueldad animal en el municipio de Eterazama, Tropico de Cochabamba, a raiz del video que circula en redes sociales donde se ve a un hombre arrastrando a dos perros atados con una cuerda en plena via publica.

En el video se ve claramente:

* Un perro ya muerto, siendo arrastrado por el suelo.
* Un segundo perro aun con vida, obligado a caminar, con la cola entre las patas y temblando de miedo.

Segun la persona que filmo el video y familiares de la denunciante, este no es un hecho aislado. Se afirma que este individuo tiene problemas de alcohol y que repetidamente consigue perros nuevos solo para maltratarlos hasta que mueren. Cuando uno muere, lo reemplaza por otro. Es decir, estamos ante un patron de crueldad sistematica, no un "caso puntual".

Bajo la Ley No. 700 para la Defensa de los Animales contra actos de Crueldad y Maltrato, y su reglamentacion mediante el Decreto Supremo 4341, lo que se ve en el video –y lo que se denuncia como practica repetida– constituye un posible delito que requiere una intervencion inmediata. La inaccion de las autoridades, sabiendo que este caso es publico y ampliamente difundido, se acerca peligrosamente a la complicidad.

Por todo lo anterior, exigimos:

1. Intervencion inmediata en el domicilio del agresor para:

   * Verificar su identidad,
   * Asegurar la proteccion y el retiro de cualquier animal actualmente bajo su "cuidado".

2. Evaluacion veterinaria urgente de cualquier perro encontrado en el lugar, con informes escritos (fotos, videos, certificados) que puedan servir como prueba en un proceso penal.

3. Apertura de una investigacion penal bajo la Ley 700, que incluya:

   * Toma formal de declaracion de la persona que grabo el video y demas testigos,
   * Incautacion del material audiovisual como evidencia,
   * Informe tecnico de Zoonosis / Pofoma sobre el estado de los animales y del entorno.

4. Imposicion de medidas restrictivas, incluyendo:

   * Prohibicion de tenencia de animales para este individuo,
   * Evaluacion psicologica, dado el riesgo evidente de reincidencia.

5. Un comunicado publico claro y actualizaciones continuas, que incluya:

   * Una comunicacion publica de la autoridad competente confirmando que este caso ha sido abierto,
   * La base legal que se esta aplicando (Ley 700 y su reglamentacion),
   * Las medidas concretas adoptadas hasta el momento (retiro de animales, pasos de investigacion, imputaciones, etc.),
   * Actualizaciones publicas periodicas conforme avance el caso.

No se trata de "un perro mas en la calle". Se trata de un hombre que, segun los propios vecinos, ha convertido la crueldad en rutina: consigue un perro, lo maltrata hasta que muere, lo arrastra como basura y luego repite el proceso. Si las autoridades permiten que esto continue a la vista de todos, el mensaje es muy claro: en Eterazama y Cochabamba maltratar animales hasta matarlos no tiene consecuencias.

Pruebas: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaOqkLhzIz5G7GBByye2K-sumDUxAKZP/view?usp=sharing

Atentamente,

To whom it may concern,

I am writing to file a formal complaint for animal cruelty in the municipality of Eterazama, Tropico de Cochabamba, based on the video circulating on social media in which a man can be seen dragging two dogs tied with a rope along a public road.

The video clearly shows:

* One dog already dead, being dragged along the ground.
* A second dog still alive, being forced to walk, tail between its legs and trembling with fear.

According to the person who filmed the video and relatives of the complainant, this is not an isolated incident. It is reported that this individual has alcohol problems and repeatedly acquires new dogs only to mistreat them until they die. When one dies, he replaces it with another. In other words, we are dealing with a pattern of systematic cruelty, not a "one-off case".

Under Law No. 700 for the Defense of Animals against Acts of Cruelty and Mistreatment, and its regulation through Supreme Decree 4341, what is seen in the video – and what is reported as a repeated practice – constitutes a possible crime that requires immediate intervention. The inaction of the authorities, knowing that this case is public and widely shared, comes dangerously close to complicity.

For these reasons, we demand:

1. Immediate intervention at the aggressor's residence in order to:

   * Verify his identity,
   * Ensure the protection and removal of any animals currently under his "care".

2. Urgent veterinary assessment of any dog found at the location, with written reports (photos, videos, certificates) that can serve as evidence in criminal proceedings.

3. Opening of a criminal investigation under Law 700, including:

   * Formal collection of the statement from the person who recorded the video and other witnesses,
   * Seizure of the audiovisual material as evidence,
   * A technical report from Zoonosis / Pofoma on the condition of the animals and the environment.

4. Imposition of restrictive measures, including:

   * A ban on animal ownership for this individual,
   * A psychological evaluation, given the obvious risk of reoffending.

5. A clear public statement and ongoing updates, including:

   * A public communication from the competent authority confirming that this case has been opened,
   * The legal basis being applied (Law 700 and its regulations),
   * The concrete measures taken so far (removal of animals, investigation steps, charges, etc.),
   * Periodic public updates as the case progresses.

This is not "just another dog in the street". This is a man who, according to his own neighbours, has turned cruelty into a routine: he gets a dog, abuses it until it dies, drags it like garbage, and then repeats the process. If the authorities allow this to continue in full view of everyone, the message is very clear: in Eterazama and Cochabamba, abusing animals to death carries no consequences.

Evidence: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1LaOqkLhzIz5G7GBByye2K-sumDUxAKZP/view?usp=sharing

Sincerely,

links.title: Puppy Neglect In Lewisville, TX
links.descriptionLong:
This poor puppy is shut out day and night. Sometimes there's a crate, sometimes there isn't. He's had no protection from the recent storms in the area.
Under Texas Penal Code §42.092, this appears to meet the definition of:
- Unreasonably failing to provide necessary shelter and care for an animal in one’s custody (§42.092(b)(3)); and
- Confining an animal in a cruel manner (§42.092(b)(5)).
The owner claims they work 12-hour shifts and “has to” shut the puppy out or he will soil the house. And yet the puppy is shut out with no shelter, whilst the owner is home.
Animal services have been called, but aren't taking the situation seriously. Forecasts in Lewisville expect temperatures of 30°F next week, cold enough to cause severe illness or death if he's shut out without shelter.
links.mailtoSubject: Puppy Neglect In Lewisville, TX
links.mailtoBody:
To whom it may concern,

I am writing about an ongoing neglect case at:

Avalon at Pier 121 Apartments
951 Leora Ln, Unit 4215
Lewisville, TX 75056

Since at least 5 November, a young dog has been routinely shut out on an apartment balcony for extended periods, including during recent severe storms. Sometimes there is a crate present; sometimes there is nothing. The balcony is exposed, and the dog has no ability to retreat indoors or escape the wind, rain and temperature extremes.

On 5 November, a complaint was filed with Lewisville Animal Services. On 10 November, an officer from Lewisville Animal Services stated the owner had been told they cannot leave the dog on the balcony, that the dog would now be kept inside, and that patrols would confirm the dog was no longer on the patio.

Despite that written assurance, the situation continues.

The owner has previously claimed they work 12-hour shifts and “has to” shut the puppy out or he will soil the house. The latest footage shows this is not about being away at work, even when someone is home, the dog is still being banished to the balcony. This is a pattern of convenience-based neglect.

Forecasts for Lewisville over the coming week show overnight lows dropping to around 30°F. If this puppy continues to be confined on an exposed balcony in those conditions, there is a real risk of hypothermia or death, not just discomfort. This is an urgent welfare concern, not something that can wait until the animal is visibly in crisis.

Under Texas Penal Code §42.092, this appears to meet the definition of:

- Unreasonably failing to provide necessary shelter and care for an animal in one’s custody (§42.092(b)(3)); and
- Confining an animal in a cruel manner (§42.092(b)(5)).

I am requesting:

1. An immediate welfare check that includes seeing the dog indoors and assessing the overall living conditions, not just a quick look from the parking lot.
2. Enforcement action, citations and, if this pattern is confirmed, criminal charges under §42.092.
3. A review of why this case was effectively treated as resolved after a promise of compliance, while balcony confinement has continued, and whether this reflects a wider pattern of non-enforcement in similar cases.
4. For Avalon at Pier 121 and AvalonBay to enforce their lease and pet policies so that no animal in their “pet-friendly” complex is used as a balcony solution to toilet training.

Given that this case involves ongoing public concern, severe weather, and an apparent failure to enforce existing law, we are calling for public statements from Lewisville Animal Services, Lewisville Police Department, and AvalonBay / Avalon at Pier 121 outlining:

- What has been done in this specific case,
- What enforcement actions will follow if the neglect continues,

Evidence: https://drive.google.com/file/d/13DKMDo20Ieyqz8GeaIsoSQldCEk_hzJT/view?usp=sharing

Sincerely,

links.mailtoSubject: Urgent Action Required for Skid Row Dogs
links.mailtoBody:
To whom it may concern,

I am writing with deep concern to demand immediate and sustained action to protect the abused and neglected animals living in the Skid Row area of Downtown Los Angeles.

While Skid Row’s human crisis rightly draws attention, we cannot ignore the silent suffering of the countless animals trapped in this humanitarian emergency, sick, starving, mistreated, and exposed to extreme elements without care or protection.

Many of these animals are left to endure:

Chronic malnutrition and dehydration

Lack of medical treatment, vaccinations, or sterilization

Violent abuse or improper restraints

Forced drug use and overdose

Long-term exposure to extreme heat or cold

Dangerous, unsanitary conditions with no oversight

These acts, whether by neglect or deliberate cruelty, are violations of California Penal Code 597, yet enforcement is virtually nonexistent. Compassion should never be selective. Animals in Skid Row are just as deserving of protection under the law as those in any other neighborhood.

We urge the City of Los Angeles to:

Create a dedicated task force to identify and intervene in cases of animal abuse and neglect on Skid Row.

Provide accessible veterinary outreach services, including food, treatment, and spay/neuter programs to pet owners in need.

Hold perpetrators of abuse accountable through appropriate penalties and interventions.

Implement co-sheltering policies so that people do not have to choose between housing and keeping their animals safe.

Animal cruelty is never excusable, regardless of circumstance. Los Angeles has the opportunity to lead with both compassion and accountability.

Please act now.

Sincerely,

links.title: Demand Animal Welfare Laws in Vietnam
links.descriptionLong: There are currently no comprehensive animal welfare laws in Vietnam. Any protections animals have are flimsy at best and nestled into other legislation, leaving plenty of wiggle room for loopholes to be exploited. Due to these exploits, dogs and cats are stolen from the streets to be slaughtered in deplorable conditions with no legal consequences. A ban on dog meat has been in effect in Hanoi since 2021, but there is no national ban yet, despite 64% of Vietnamese people supporting one.
